title: Retry backoff becomes exponential; config key renamed
intent_summary: Replaces the fixed 1s retry delay with capped exponential backoff so a struggling upstream is not hammered at a constant rate, and renames the RETRY_DELAY config key to RETRY_BASE_DELAY to match the new meaning. A drive-by extracts the delay math into a helper and regenerates the lockfile.

threads.title: Exponential backoff
threads.summary: The retry loop's delay policy changes from fixed to exponential with a 60s cap — the behavioral heart of the branch. Read this first: everything else is consequence.

steps.summary: Retries now back off exponentially from RETRY_BASE_DELAY, doubling per attempt, capped at 60s.
steps.detail: Before: every retry waited a constant 1s. After: the wait is base * 2**attempt, clamped to 60s. A caller that used to retry 5 times in 5s now spreads those retries across roughly a minute.
steps.why_now: Start here — this is the observable behavior change the whole branch exists to make; every other step is downstream of it.
review_prompts: Compare the old constant-delay line with the new base * 2**attempt computation: is the cap applied before or after the doubling, so a single wait can never exceed 60s?

attention_notes.text: No test in the diff exercises the new timing — the behavior change is unverified by the suite.
The stated goal asked for 'exponential backoff with jitter'; no thread adds jitter, so the schedule is fully deterministic. Deliberate, or unserved?

steps.summary: Whether the 60s cap is observable depends on the caller's own request timeout, which this diff does not show.
steps.why_now: Read right after the policy change: if the pool's per-request timeout is under 60s, the cap never bites and the change is smaller than it looks.
review_prompts: Check src/client/pool.py's request timeout: is it shorter than 60s? If so, does the new cap ever actually take effect?

evidence.note: widened: src/client/pool.py sets the per-request timeout but was not changed by this branch

threads.title: Config key rename (drive-by)
threads.summary: RETRY_DELAY becomes RETRY_BASE_DELAY. Mechanical to write, but it is a public config surface — compatibility is the question, not the code.

steps.summary: The old RETRY_DELAY key is renamed to RETRY_BASE_DELAY and no longer read; a deployment still setting the old key silently loses its override.
steps.detail: The config loader reads only the new name. There is no compatibility shim or deprecation warning, so an existing RETRY_DELAY in the environment is now ignored rather than honored.
steps.why_now: Read after the backoff change it supports: this is where the change reaches operators, and the compatibility break lives here, not in the retry math.
review_prompts: Confirm no deployment target still sets RETRY_DELAY (env, deploy templates, runbooks); should the old key be honored for one release with a warning?

threads.title: Extract the delay helper
threads.summary: The delay computation moves out of the retry loop into a compute_delay() helper. Pure extraction — the arithmetic is unchanged; read it to confirm that.

steps.summary: The doubling-and-cap arithmetic is lifted verbatim into compute_delay(); the retry loop now calls the helper.
steps.detail: The extracted body is the same expression, in the same order (double, then clamp). No new branch, no changed constant. The intent is a readability refactor with no behavior change.
steps.why_now: Read alongside the backoff change: this is the same math relocated, so confirming it here lets you trust t1.s1 without re-reading the arithmetic twice.
review_prompts: Confirm the extracted compute_delay() preserves the order of operations — cap applied *after* doubling, exactly as the inline version did — so the refactor changes nothing observable.

threads.title: Test and lockfile (drive-by)
threads.summary: A new timing test and a regenerated lockfile ride along. The test documents the backoff; the lockfile is pure churn.

steps.summary: A new test asserts the delay sequence 1, 2, 4, 8, … and that it never exceeds 60s.
steps.why_now: Read this once you understand t1.s1: it is the evidence that the new behavior is pinned, and it is the first thing that would fail if the cap regressed.
review_prompts: Does this test fail if the 60s cap is removed, or only if the doubling breaks? A test that passes with the cap gone is not pinning the cap.

steps.summary: poetry.lock is regenerated with no dependency version changes — hash churn only.
steps.why_now: Skim last, or skip: nothing here carries narrative, and no dependency actually moved.

evidence.note: poetry.lock is listed as changed but the classifier omitted its body as lockfile churn
